// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 7 — HOOKS: THE ENFORCEMENT LAYER

This chapter gives a working overview of Hooks: The Enforcement Layer, focusing on the ideas a reader needs before movin

Chapter 7 — Hooks: The Enforcement Layer — A CLAUDE.md instruction that says "never generate a final grade" is a request. A Hook that blocks grade generation is en
NARRATION

This chapter gives a working overview of Hooks: The Enforcement Layer, focusing on the ideas a reader needs before moving to the next chapter. The chapter moves through Learning outcomes, Opening, What a Hook is, The four most useful events for teachers, and related ideas. Read it for the main argument, the vocabulary it introduces, and the practical judgment it asks you to develop. A CLAUDE.md instruction that says "never generate a final grade" is a request. A Hook that blocks grade generation is enforcement.

// SLIDE 02 — OPENING

OPENING

The teacher had added to CLAUDE.md, in capital letters, under a NEVER heading:

Opening — NEVER generate a final grade. The tool produces patterns and flags only. Numeric and letter grades are the teacher's res
NARRATION

The teacher had added to CLAUDE.md, in capital letters, under a NEVER heading: NEVER generate a final grade. The tool produces patterns and flags only. Numeric and letter grades are the teacher's responsibility.

// SLIDE 03 — THE FOUR MOST USEFUL EVENTS FO

THE FOUR MOST USEFUL EVENTS FOR TEACHERS.

PreToolUse.→PostToolUse.→SessionStart.→SessionEnd.
NARRATION

Claude Code's 2026 hook system supports many lifecycle points. The four that cover most teacher use: PreToolUse. Runs before Claude uses a tool. The hook receives the proposed tool input on stdin. The hook can approve (exit 0), block (exit non-zero with error), or modify (return modified input). This is the primary enforcement point , the chapter's grade-generation block is a PreToolUse hook on Write.

// SLIDE 04 — CLAUDE.MD VS. HOOK: WHICH RULE

CLAUDE.MD VS. HOOK: WHICH RULE BELONGS WHERE

Enforcement — Advisory (Claude tries to follow)
Cost — Context space; small per-prompt
Reliability — High, not absolute
Updatable — Edit the markdown file
NARRATION

The heuristic: if violating this rule would be a real problem, it is a Hook. If violating this rule would be annoying, it is a CLAUDE.md entry. For the grading tool, the rules that must hold every time are the ones with student-facing consequences: never generate a final grade; never modify a submission file; never write outside grading/. Each becomes a hook.

// SLIDE 05 — THE "ASK CLAUDE TO WRITE THE H

THE "ASK CLAUDE TO WRITE THE HOOK" PATTERN.

Hooks feel intimidating. The script syntax is unfamiliar. The lifecycle-event configuration is documentation-heavy. Most

The "ask Claude to write the hook" patte — The pattern: ask Claude to write the hook. "Write a PreToolUse hook in .claude/hooks/block-grades.sh that blocks any Wri
NARRATION

Hooks feel intimidating. The script syntax is unfamiliar. The lifecycle-event configuration is documentation-heavy. Most teachers will not learn to write hooks from scratch. The pattern: ask Claude to write the hook. "Write a PreToolUse hook in .claude/hooks/block-grades.sh that blocks any Write where the proposed content contains a numeric grade pattern (percentages, letter grades, x/y fractions, 'out of' phrasing). The hook should exit non-zero with a teacher-readable error message. Register it in .claude/settings.json."

// SLIDE 06 — THREE HOOKS FOR THE GRADING TO

THREE HOOKS FOR THE GRADING TOOL.

The grading tool's three priorities, as hooks.

Three hooks for the grading tool — The opening's failure mode. The hook above, plus the settings.json registration. Test by explicitly asking Claude "Write
NARRATION

The grading tool's three priorities, as hooks. The opening's failure mode. The hook above, plus the settings.json registration. Test by explicitly asking Claude "Write Sara's grade report including her overall letter grade." The hook fires; the Write is blocked; Claude reports the block; no file is written with a grade.

// SLIDE 07 — WHAT HOOKS CANNOT ENFORCE

WHAT HOOKS CANNOT ENFORCE.

Conversational manipulation.→Out-of-band actions.→The wrong abstraction.
NARRATION

The honest claim. Hooks are deterministic for the events they cover. Some things are outside the events. Conversational manipulation. A user can talk Claude into reasoning toward a wrong answer; if the wrong answer does not trigger a tool call that the hook covers, the hook does not fire. Hooks protect file system writes, not the conversation itself.

// SLIDE 08 — A WORKED TABLE: CLAUDE.MD VS.

A WORKED TABLE: CLAUDE.MD VS. HOOK

Grade generation block — "Never generate a final grade"
CSS isolation during HTML edits — "Don't change CSS when editing HTML"
Submission protection — "Never touch submissions/"
Verification run — "Run lint after every edit"
NARRATION

Read the two columns. The left column is what most teachers write when they first encounter Claude Code. The right column is what they end up writing after they have lived through the failure mode the left column did not catch. The conversion is not always one-to-one. Some CLAUDE.md entries (style preferences, lessons learned) stay in CLAUDE.md because the enforcement does not need to be deterministic. The chapter is not about moving everything; it is about moving the things that must hold.

// SLIDE 09 — TEACHER BUILD: THREE HOOKS FOR

TEACHER BUILD: THREE HOOKS FOR YOUR GRADING TOOL

Hook 1block final grade generation. Ask Claude to write the PreToolUse hook. Review the script. Register in settings.json. Com
Hook 2verification after edit. Ask Claude to write the PostToolUse hook. Verification script checks for grade patterns and str
Hook 3session-end audit log. Ask Claude to write the SessionEnd hook. Logs file paths, tools used, tokens, duration. Commit. T
NARRATION

Apply the chapter's discipline to your grading-tool build. Hook 1 , block final grade generation. Ask Claude to write the PreToolUse hook. Review the script. Register in settings.json. Commit. Test by explicitly asking for a grade; verify the block fires.

// SLIDE 10 — CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: STUDENTS C

CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: STUDENTS CONVERT ADVISORY RULES TO HOOKS

Provide a project with three CLAUDE.md rules that need stronger enforcement. (Example: "never delete files outside temp/

Classroom activity: students convert adv — Each student selects one rule and converts it to a hook by asking Claude to write the hook. They review the generated sc
NARRATION

Provide a project with three CLAUDE.md rules that need stronger enforcement. (Example: "never delete files outside temp/"; "always run the linter after edits"; "never write secrets to source.") Each student selects one rule and converts it to a hook by asking Claude to write the hook. They review the generated script line by line. They test the hook by deliberately attempting the prohibited action. They submit the script, the settings.json change, the test transcript, and a one-paragraph explanation of why this rule needs hook-level enforcement.

// SLIDE 11 — AI WAYBACK MACHINE

AI WAYBACK MACHINE.

Claude Shannon (1916–2001) — American mathematician and electrical engineer whose 1937 MIT master's thesis "A Symbolic A

AI Wayback Machine — A Hook is a Shannon gate for Claude Code. The CLAUDE.md instruction is the advisory — what Claude is told to do, process
NARRATION

Claude Shannon (1916, 2001) , American mathematician and electrical engineer whose 1937 MIT master's thesis "A Symbolic Analysis of Relay and Switching Circuits" established that boolean logic could be implemented as physical mechanisms , relays, transistors, eventually integrated circuits , that enforce boolean conditions regardless of context, regardless of intent, regardless of who is using the system. Shannon's later work in "A Mathematical Theory of Communication" (1948) founded information theory; the 1937 thesis was the operational foundation that made digital computing possible.

// SLIDE 12 — THESIS

Shannon's later work in "A Mathematical Theory of Communication" (1948) founded information theory; the 1937 thesis was the operational foundation that made digital computing possible.
